title: My Ebike Delivery Went Missing. When I Tried to Recover It, I Ended Up in Chatbot Hell
gist: A first-person account of a lost $2,000 ebike delivery turning into months of chatbot-routed dead ends across FedEx, a bank, a card company, and the local police, framed against survey data showing 31% of customer service leaders are already cutting headcount for AI and 59-85% of consumers are frustrated with or prefer to avoid AI customer service agents.

insights.claim: An April 2026 survey of customer service leaders found 31% have already reduced or plan to reduce headcount due to AI adoption, though a majority say they are redeploying human agents to new roles rather than outright layoffs.
insights.speaker: Dillon Thompson
insights.so_what: Quantifies the pace of AI-driven headcount reduction in customer service roles, a leading indicator for labor-market and BPO-industry disruption.
insights.quote: 31 percent said they have already reduced or are planning to reduce headcount due to AI adoption
insights.locator: In a survey of customer service leaders published in April

insights.claim: Verizon CEO Dan Schulman said AI will likely replace a 'large percentage' of the company's customer service workforce, naming customer service as one of the business functions most exposed to AI-driven change.
insights.speaker: Dillon Thompson
insights.so_what: A direct executive-level data point on AI-driven labor substitution at a major telecom, relevant for modeling opex and labor-cost trajectories at large customer-service-heavy employers.
insights.quote: AI will likely replace a "large percentage" of the company's customer service work
insights.locator: Some leaders have been more brazen, though

insights.claim: A May 2026 survey of US, UK, and Canadian consumers found 59% are frustrated with AI customer service agents and 85% would prefer to speak with a real person, signaling a wide gap between corporate AI-adoption enthusiasm and actual consumer sentiment.
insights.speaker: Dillon Thompson
insights.so_what: A quantified consumer-backlash data point that could translate into churn and brand-loyalty risk for companies aggressively automating service, and a differentiation opportunity for firms that preserve human support.
insights.quote: 59 percent said they were frustrated with AI customer service agents. Meanwhile, 85 percent said they'd prefer to speak with a real person.
insights.locator: Whether it's due to organically bad experiences

insights.claim: Marketing professor Ryan Hamilton (Emory) argues AI has amplified a pre-existing corporate tactic called 'sludge,' deliberately adding friction to customer service to discourage people from pursuing refunds or complaints, rather than creating a new problem.
insights.speaker: Dillon Thompson
insights.so_what: Provides an analytical lens for evaluating whether a company's AI customer-service deployment is a genuine efficiency play or a disguised cost-avoidance tactic, relevant to consumer-protection and reputational-risk assessment.
insights.quote: Sludge existed before AI... But AI, like with everything else, has just sort of ramped up the dystopian nature of it.
insights.locator: As Ryan Hamilton, a marketing professor

insights.claim: Hamilton notes some companies knowingly accept degraded customer experience from AI deployment as a deliberate trade-off, while others operate in industries where poor service is not survivable, implying AI customer-service outcomes will bifurcate by competitive intensity of the industry.
insights.speaker: Dillon Thompson
insights.so_what: A segmentation lens: expect AI-service backlash to hit hardest in competitive, low-switching-cost consumer industries, while monopolistic or high-switching-cost industries like shipping, telecom, and banking can sustain worse AI-driven service longer.
insights.quote: Sometimes they're aware... They're willing to accept that trade-off.
insights.locator: Hamilton says that some companies adopt technologies

themes.theme: Competitive Differentiation Risk from AI Homogenization

insights.claim: Hamilton warns broad AI customer-service adoption risks 'smoothing out the service dimension' so companies across every industry converge on effectively the same AI call-center experience, eliminating a traditional axis of competitive differentiation.
insights.speaker: Dillon Thompson
insights.so_what: Implies companies willing to invest in superior human or hybrid service could gain a durable competitive and brand advantage precisely because AI-only competitors are becoming commoditized and indistinguishable.
insights.quote: smoothing out the service dimension... where everyone is going to have the same AI call center, no matter what industry you're in
insights.locator: There's a risk these changes are

themes.theme: Sunk-Cost Dynamics & Investor Pressure Behind AI Rollouts

insights.claim: Yale's Ravi Dhar attributes aggressive corporate AI rollout partly to a sunk-cost dynamic, where executives feel 'pot-committed' to AI spending because investors and Wall Street are demanding a visible AI strategy and ROI, independent of whether deployments are actually working.
insights.speaker: Dillon Thompson
insights.so_what: Identifies investor pressure itself as a causal driver of poor-quality AI rollouts, a reflexive dynamic worth weighing when evaluating whether a company's AI capex announcements reflect genuine efficiency gains or defensive signaling to markets.
insights.quote: If you're a CEO, you're getting questions from all of the investors, from Wall Street, like, 'Hey, what is your AI strategy, first of all, and is it showing any return on investment? You're spending all this money.'
insights.locator: Ravi Dhar, a professor at Yale

insights.claim: Agentic AI capable of autonomous tool-calling, exemplified by Meta's Toolformer, is only about three years old, underscoring that current customer-service AI agents are still an early-stage technology despite being deployed at mass commercial scale.
insights.speaker: Dillon Thompson
insights.so_what: A timeline anchor showing companies are deploying agentic AI in mission-critical, customer-facing roles well ahead of the technology's maturity curve, a risk factor for service quality and reputational outcomes.
insights.quote: Meta's Toolformer, a model that was considered groundbreaking for its ability to call upon external tools when in doubt, is just three years old.
